video_title: Angular momentum of rigid systems | AP Physics | Khan Academy

segments.text:
- [Instructor] Here's a
ruler kept on a table.
Suppose I flick it over here,
what's gonna happen?
Well, it's gonna go forward
and it's gonna spin a bit, right?
So when I flick it,
the ruler gets both a translational motion
and a rotational motion.

So the question we wanna try
to answer in this video is
how do we predict how much linear

and angular velocity it
gains after I flick it?

You may be wondering, why
should we care about that?

Well, over here, maybe
it's not so high stakes,

but what if you consider
an orbiting satellite?

Maybe we want to change
its orientation a bit,
or maybe it's spinning uncontrollably
and we want to stop it remotely.
Ooh, now we need to know
how to compute all of this stuff, right?

So over here, we're gonna
learn how to do that
by introducing a pretty cool idea
called angular momentum.
So let's begin.
So how do we compute this?

Well, we know that this
ruler is undergoing

both a translational
and rotational motion.

But the cool thing is we
can analyze them separately.

So let's first look at
the translational motion
and then try to analogously think
about rotational motion, okay?
So when it comes to translational motion,
how do I figure out how much velocity
this ruler has gained after I flipped it?

Well, I can think in
terms of linear momentum.
Remember, linear momentum of any object

equals the mass times the
velocity of the object.
So if I could somehow figure out
what the linear momentum of this ruler is
after I flick it, I'm done.
Because then I can divide by the mass

and I will find what its
velocity is going to be.
So I can use the idea of linear momentum
to compute the linear velocity.
Now, similarly, when it comes to rotation,
we can define
a quantity called angular momentum.
It is analogous to linear momentum,

just like how linear
momentum is a measure of

how much motion, how much
translational motion,
an object has,
we can think of angular momentum

as a measure of how much
rotational motion an object has.
And how do we define it?
How do we write an expression for it?
We can write it analogously.

So it would be a great
idea to pause the video
and see if you can first make a guess
of what this expression is going to be.
Okay, so over here,

linear momentum equals
mass times velocity, right?
Well, instead of mass,

what's analogous to mass
when it comes to rotation?
Well, that's rotational inertia, right?

And what's analogous to
velocity in rotation?
Hey, that's angular velocity.

So we define angular
momentum as the product

of rotational inertia
and angular velocity.
Let's see if this makes intuitive sense.
If you increase angular velocity,
the angular momentum increases.
Does that make sense?
Yeah.
If an object starts spinning faster,
well, it has more rotational motion,
so it makes sense to say
that it has more angular momentum.
But the expression also tells us,
if you increase the rotational inertia,
that too increases the angular momentum.
Does that make sense?
This is a little subtle.

How do you increase
the rotational inertia?
Remember, rotational inertia is a measure
of how a mass is distributed, right?

For example, consider two
spheres attached by a light rod.

If the mass of the spheres
are exactly the same,

both of these objects have
exactly the same mass.

But if you're spinning them
about their centers over here,

they don't have the
same rotational inertia.

Over here, the masses are
very close to the center,
so it has lower rotational inertia.
And over here, the mass is just, you know,
it's farther away from the center
so it has a higher rotational inertia.
So if you were to spin both of them
with the same angular velocity,

then this one ends up having
more angular momentum.
Does that make sense?
Well, yeah.
I mean, look at this.

If the mass is farther
away from the center,
it moves faster.

Notice that these
spheres are moving faster
compared to this one,

even though they're moving
with the same angular speed.
They have the same RPM,
but because they're farther apart,

those spheres are farther
apart, they're moving faster.
So it makes sense to think
that this one has more rotational motion
compared to this one,

and therefore this one
had more angular momentum
compared to this one.

Okay, next up, let's
think about the direction.
Just like how both linear momentum
and velocity are vector quantities,
angular momentum and angular velocities
are also vector quantities.
So how do we think about their direction?
Let's start with the angular velocity.
If a disc is spinning,
let's say this particular way,

then what is the direction
of the angular velocity?

Well, we use something
called the right hand rule.
Over here, we use our four fingers

and we curl it in the
direction of the spin.
And then the thumb points to the direction
of the angular velocity.
So in this particular case,

the direction of angular
velocity is upwards.

So you can imagine if it was
spinning the other way around,

then its angular velocity
would be downwards.
Okay, so that's angular velocity.
What's the direction of angular momentum?
Well, we might look at this and we say,

hey, over here, momentum has
the same direction as velocity.
So could we also say that angular momentum

has the same direction
as angular velocity?
Ooh, this is subtle.
It turns out not to be so in general.
Now in special cases,
when we are dealing with rotation around
something called the principal axis,
which is basically an axis of symmetry,
then the angular momentum
does happen to be in the same direction
as angular velocity,
but that's a special case.
So for example, in this particular case,
this is our principal axis.
So over here in this specific example,
the angular momentum will also be
in the same direction as angular velocity.
Here is another principal axis.
So if you were to spin the disc
along this particular axis,
kind of like when we flip a coin, right?
Then it turns out this is also
a principal axis of rotation,
meaning along this axis,

again, angular momentum
and angular velocity,
will be in the same direction.
So with the direction over here,
well, again, we use our right hand rule.

We make sure that our
four fingers are curling
in the direction of rotations.
Over here, we have to call it this way,

and so the angular velocity
will be to the left.
And so in this particular case,

the angular momentum
will also be to the left.

But what's important is
that the rotational inertia
along this axis would be different
compared to that along this axis,

because the distribution
of mass is different.
In fact, along this axis,
it turns out that the rotational inertia

is actually lower compared
to along this axis.
And so even if I were to spin it
with the same omega along this axis,

the angular momentum would
have a smaller magnitude.

Okay, so these two were
the principal axis, right?

So the angular momentum
and the angular velocity
were having the same direction.
But now consider this.
What if this disc was spinning both
along this and this axis simultaneously?
Then its angular velocity would be
a vector sum of these two, right?
It'll be this way.

And so the angular momentum
would be vector sum
of these two, and it would be that way.
And look, the angular momentum now
is not pointing in the same direction
as the angular velocity.
So this is not a principal axis.
Along this axis of rotation,

the angular momentum
and the angular velocity
are not in the same direction.
So long story short,

whenever we are dealing
with principal axis,

these two are always
in the same direction,
and rotational inertia becomes
just a simple number, a scaler.

However, if you're dealing
with non-principal axes,

then they're not aligned
in the same direction,
then the rotational inertia
is no longer a scaler, not a vector,

it becomes something
called rank-two tensor.
But we don't have to worry about that
because we're gonna restrict our analysis
to principal axis only.
So for our case, the angular momentum
and angular velocity
will point in the same direction,
and rotational inertia is just a scaler.
Okay, coming back to our original problem,

if I could figure out
how much angular momentum

this ruler has gained after
I flick it, then I'm done.

Because then I can divide
by the moment of inertia
and calculate the angular velocity.
But the big question now is

how do I figure out how much
angular momentum it gains
after I flick it?

Well, let's come back
from translational motion.

How do we figure out how
much linear momentum it gains
after we flick it?
Well, we can use Newton second law.
According to Newton second law,
the net force acting on an object
equals mass times its acceleration.
But what exactly is acceleration?
Well, it's a measure of how quickly
the velocity is changing.
It's the rate of change of velocity.
So I can plug that in.

So we can write this as M
times delta V over delta T.

Now, strictly speaking,
we should call this
the average rate of change of velocity,
because this value is averaged
over that time interval delta T.

So this is average acceleration
over the time interval,

and this is the average net
force over that time interval.
But I'm gonna stop using the word average
over and over again,

because I wanna talk
about this intuitively.
Intuitively speaking, acceleration is

a measure of how quickly the
velocity is changing, right?

Okay, now, if I pull this
mass inside the delta,
I'll get delta MV over delta T.
What is MV?
Hey, that's momentum.
So this becomes delta P over delta T.

In other words, this is
rate of change, of momentum.
Again, average, okay?
So this equation is saying

that the net force acting
on an object decides
how quickly its momentum changes.
And this is exactly what I wanted,
because I am interested in delta P.

So now I can use this
equation to rearrange

and write it as delta
P equals the net force,
the average net force,

over the time into
(indistinct) times delta T.
And this product of the net force

multiplied by the time over
which that net force is acting,
We call it the impulse.
So this means that all I need to know

is the impulse delivered
by my finger on this ruler.
If I know that, I'm done,

because that equals
the change in momentum.

And once I know how much
the momentum has changed,
I can figure out the final momentum,
and I can figure out the final velocity
after I have flicked this ruler.

Alright, now let's do the
same thing for rotation.
In fact, I want you to pause the video
and see if you can try to write down
Newton's second law for rotation yourself.
It's gonna be analogous to this,
so please first try it yourself.
Okay, let's do that.
Over here, we were dealing with net force.
Over here, it's going to be net torque.

And over here, that equals
mass times acceleration.

Here, instead of mass, we
will have rotational inertia,
and instead of acceleration,
we will have angular acceleration.
What's angular acceleration?
Well, linear acceleration is a measure
of how quickly linear velocity changes.
Angular acceleration is a measure of

So this equals rotational inertia

this is the average rate of
change of angular velocity,

so this is the average
angular acceleration,
so this is the average net torque.
But anyways, just like before,

inertia inside the delta,

In other words, how quickly

depends on the net torque acting on it.
Again, average values, okay?

momentum has changed.

So just like before, we

Here, we have an analogous

We call this the angular impulse,

how much angular impulse my
finger delivered to this ruler.
Once I know that, I am done.

That equals how much the
angular momentum has changed.
And from that, I can calculate

And from there, I can calculate

once I have flicked this ruler.
So putting it all together,
when I flick this ruler,
my force imparted both an impulse
and an angular impulse on that ruler.

As a result, it gained both
linear and angular momentum.

So it started moving
and spinning together.

But what if I had flicked
the ruler over here?

We can intuitively guess
now it wouldn't spin,
it would just go forward.
But why?
Well, because this time, again,
my force delivers an impulse,

but it no longer delivers
an angular impulse,
because this time, the force acts through
the center of mass,

and as a result, there
is no torque generated.
So torque is zero.

The net torque acting

angular impulse is zero.
Therefore, this time,

That's why this time, the

Finally,
if I were to draw a graph of the net force

the signed area under that graph.
So that total area would represents

Similarly, if I were to plot a graph
